symptom_text: gateway on different subnet than device
solutions: Check device IP and mask: ipconfig /all or ip addr | Identify gateway IP and its subnet | Verify device subnet contains the gateway IP (use subnet calculator) | Example correct config: device 192.168.1.50/24, gateway 192.168.1.1 | Set correct subnet mask: 255.255.255.0 for /24 networks | If using static IP, reconfigure with matching subnet | Check DHCP server subnet option — it may be misconfigured | Restart network after fixing subnet to apply changes

symptom_text: routing table missing default route entry
solutions: Verify gateway IP: route print (Windows) or ip route show (Linux) | Ping gateway: ping <gateway_ip> — confirm it is unreachable | Re-enter correct default gateway in network settings | Check if gateway device is powered on and its port is active | Restart your network adapter | Check routing table for stale or conflicting routes | Reset TCP/IP stack: netsh int ip reset (Windows) | Verify no firewall rule is blocking ICMP to gateway

symptom_text: lost internet after power outage
solutions: Check if router is powered on — verify all indicator lights | Power cycle: unplug router for 30s then reconnect | Check WAN/Internet cable between router and modem | Verify ISP service is active — call ISP or check status page | Check router WAN IP in admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1) | Update router firmware from manufacturer website | Review router logs for PPPoE or DHCP errors from ISP | Factory reset router if all else fails

symptom_text: alternate DNS like 8.8.8.8 works but default does not
solutions: Test DNS timeout: nslookup -timeout=5 google.com | Switch to a faster DNS server (1.1.1.1 is fastest globally) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Check if UDP port 53 is blocked by local firewall | Ping 1.1.1.1 — if that works but DNS times out, ISP is throttling DNS | Contact ISP — their DNS resolver may be degraded | Enable DNS over HTTPS/TLS to bypass ISP interference | Restart router to reset DNS cache and forwarder state

symptom_text: subnet does not match gateway subnet
solutions: Check device IP and mask: ipconfig /all or ip addr | Identify gateway IP and its subnet | Verify device subnet contains the gateway IP (use subnet calculator) | Example correct config: device 192.168.1.50/24, gateway 192.168.1.1 | Set correct subnet mask: 255.255.255.0 for /24 networks | If using static IP, reconfigure with matching subnet | Check DHCP server subnet option — it may be misconfigured | Restart network after fixing subnet to apply changes

symptom_text: dhcp server not responding to discovery
solutions: Verify DHCP is enabled in router admin panel | Release and renew: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew | Power cycle router — 30s off clears DHCP server state | Check DHCP pool is not exhausted (add more addresses) | Temporarily assign a static IP to confirm it is a DHCP issue | Check if device is in a VLAN that can reach the DHCP server | Increase DHCP lease time in router settings | Reset router DHCP scope to defaults

symptom_text: ISP dns server responding too slowly
solutions: Test DNS timeout: nslookup -timeout=5 google.com | Switch to a faster DNS server (1.1.1.1 is fastest globally) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Check if UDP port 53 is blocked by local firewall | Ping 1.1.1.1 — if that works but DNS times out, ISP is throttling DNS | Contact ISP — their DNS resolver may be degraded | Enable DNS over HTTPS/TLS to bypass ISP interference | Restart router to reset DNS cache and forwarder state

symptom_text: ip conflict error shown by operating system
solutions: Identify conflicting device: arp -a | look for duplicate MACs | Release IP: ipconfig /release (Windows) or sudo dhclient -r | Renew IP: ipconfig /renew or sudo dhclient | If using static IP, move it outside DHCP pool range | Set DHCP reservations in router to prevent future conflicts | Check DHCP pool size — expand if needed | Restart router to clear stale DHCP lease table | Reserve MAC-to-IP bindings for all static devices in router

symptom_text: network interface offline after restart
solutions: Open Device Manager — check for yellow ! on network adapter | Right-click adapter → Enable if disabled | Check physical cable connection — try a different cable | Update driver: Device Manager → Update driver | Uninstall adapter in Device Manager then reboot to reinstall | Try a different USB or PCIe NIC to isolate hardware failure | Run Windows Network Diagnostics or Linux ethtool -t eth0 | Replace NIC if driver reinstall does not resolve it

symptom_text: network adapter shows limited connectivity
solutions: Verify DHCP is enabled in router admin panel | Release and renew: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew | Power cycle router — 30s off clears DHCP server state | Check DHCP pool is not exhausted (add more addresses) | Temporarily assign a static IP to confirm it is a DHCP issue | Check if device is in a VLAN that can reach the DHCP server | Increase DHCP lease time in router settings | Reset router DHCP scope to defaults

symptom_text: reset router but still no internet
solutions: Check if router is powered on — verify all indicator lights | Power cycle: unplug router for 30s then reconnect | Check WAN/Internet cable between router and modem | Verify ISP service is active — call ISP or check status page | Check router WAN IP in admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1) | Update router firmware from manufacturer website | Review router logs for PPPoE or DHCP errors from ISP | Factory reset router if all else fails

symptom_text: dns resolution failed error message
solutions: Change DNS to Google (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Flush DNS cache — macOS: sudo dscacheutil -flushcache | Test with nslookup google.com to confirm DNS failure | Restart network adapter to clear DNS state | Check router DNS settings in admin panel | Try alternate DNS: Quad9 (9.9.9.9) or OpenDNS (208.67.222.222) | Disable DNS-over-HTTPS temporarily to test

symptom_text: high latency on all dns queries
solutions: Test DNS timeout: nslookup -timeout=5 google.com | Switch to a faster DNS server (1.1.1.1 is fastest globally) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Check if UDP port 53 is blocked by local firewall | Ping 1.1.1.1 — if that works but DNS times out, ISP is throttling DNS | Contact ISP — their DNS resolver may be degraded | Enable DNS over HTTPS/TLS to bypass ISP interference | Restart router to reset DNS cache and forwarder state

symptom_text: no packets getting through to ISP
solutions: Check if router is powered on — verify all indicator lights | Power cycle: unplug router for 30s then reconnect | Check WAN/Internet cable between router and modem | Verify ISP service is active — call ISP or check status page | Check router WAN IP in admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1) | Update router firmware from manufacturer website | Review router logs for PPPoE or DHCP errors from ISP | Factory reset router if all else fails

symptom_text: broadcast address conflicts with gateway
solutions: Check device IP and mask: ipconfig /all or ip addr | Identify gateway IP and its subnet | Verify device subnet contains the gateway IP (use subnet calculator) | Example correct config: device 192.168.1.50/24, gateway 192.168.1.1 | Set correct subnet mask: 255.255.255.0 for /24 networks | If using static IP, reconfigure with matching subnet | Check DHCP server subnet option — it may be misconfigured | Restart network after fixing subnet to apply changes

symptom_text: routing table missing default route entry
solutions: Verify gateway IP: route print (Windows) or ip route show (Linux) | Ping gateway: ping <gateway_ip> — confirm it is unreachable | Re-enter correct default gateway in network settings | Check if gateway device is powered on and its port is active | Restart your network adapter | Check routing table for stale or conflicting routes | Reset TCP/IP stack: netsh int ip reset (Windows) | Verify no firewall rule is blocking ICMP to gateway

symptom_text: ip address already in use on this network
solutions: Identify conflicting device: arp -a | look for duplicate MACs | Release IP: ipconfig /release (Windows) or sudo dhclient -r | Renew IP: ipconfig /renew or sudo dhclient | If using static IP, move it outside DHCP pool range | Set DHCP reservations in router to prevent future conflicts | Check DHCP pool size — expand if needed | Restart router to clear stale DHCP lease table | Reserve MAC-to-IP bindings for all static devices in router

symptom_text: ethernet port not working at all
solutions: Open Device Manager — check for yellow ! on network adapter | Right-click adapter → Enable if disabled | Check physical cable connection — try a different cable | Update driver: Device Manager → Update driver | Uninstall adapter in Device Manager then reboot to reinstall | Try a different USB or PCIe NIC to isolate hardware failure | Run Windows Network Diagnostics or Linux ethtool -t eth0 | Replace NIC if driver reinstall does not resolve it

symptom_text: cannot get valid ip from access point
solutions: Verify DHCP is enabled in router admin panel | Release and renew: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew | Power cycle router — 30s off clears DHCP server state | Check DHCP pool is not exhausted (add more addresses) | Temporarily assign a static IP to confirm it is a DHCP issue | Check if device is in a VLAN that can reach the DHCP server | Increase DHCP lease time in router settings | Reset router DHCP scope to defaults

symptom_text: internet was working then suddenly dropped
solutions: Check if router is powered on — verify all indicator lights | Power cycle: unplug router for 30s then reconnect | Check WAN/Internet cable between router and modem | Verify ISP service is active — call ISP or check status page | Check router WAN IP in admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1) | Update router firmware from manufacturer website | Review router logs for PPPoE or DHCP errors from ISP | Factory reset router if all else fails

symptom_text: NIC not detected in bios or operating system
solutions: Open Device Manager — check for yellow ! on network adapter | Right-click adapter → Enable if disabled | Check physical cable connection — try a different cable | Update driver: Device Manager → Update driver | Uninstall adapter in Device Manager then reboot to reinstall | Try a different USB or PCIe NIC to isolate hardware failure | Run Windows Network Diagnostics or Linux ethtool -t eth0 | Replace NIC if driver reinstall does not resolve it

symptom_text: router stuck in boot loop
solutions: Check if router is powered on — verify all indicator lights | Power cycle: unplug router for 30s then reconnect | Check WAN/Internet cable between router and modem | Verify ISP service is active — call ISP or check status page | Check router WAN IP in admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1) | Update router firmware from manufacturer website | Review router logs for PPPoE or DHCP errors from ISP | Factory reset router if all else fails

symptom_text: routing problem preventing gateway access
solutions: Verify gateway IP: route print (Windows) or ip route show (Linux) | Ping gateway: ping <gateway_ip> — confirm it is unreachable | Re-enter correct default gateway in network settings | Check if gateway device is powered on and its port is active | Restart your network adapter | Check routing table for stale or conflicting routes | Reset TCP/IP stack: netsh int ip reset (Windows) | Verify no firewall rule is blocking ICMP to gateway

symptom_text: dns resolution failed error message
solutions: Change DNS to Google (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Flush DNS cache — macOS: sudo dscacheutil -flushcache | Test with nslookup google.com to confirm DNS failure | Restart network adapter to clear DNS state | Check router DNS settings in admin panel | Try alternate DNS: Quad9 (9.9.9.9) or OpenDNS (208.67.222.222) | Disable DNS-over-HTTPS temporarily to test

symptom_text: dhcp offer never received from server
solutions: Verify DHCP is enabled in router admin panel | Release and renew: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew | Power cycle router — 30s off clears DHCP server state | Check DHCP pool is not exhausted (add more addresses) | Temporarily assign a static IP to confirm it is a DHCP issue | Check if device is in a VLAN that can reach the DHCP server | Increase DHCP lease time in router settings | Reset router DHCP scope to defaults

solutions: Check if router is powered on — verify all indicator lights | Power cycle: unplug router for 30s then reconnect | Check WAN/Internet cable between router and modem | Verify ISP service is active — call ISP or check status page | Check router WAN IP in admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1) | Update router firmware from manufacturer website | Review router logs for PPPoE or DHCP errors from ISP | Factory reset router if all else fails

symptom_text: ISP dns server responding too slowly
solutions: Test DNS timeout: nslookup -timeout=5 google.com | Switch to a faster DNS server (1.1.1.1 is fastest globally) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Check if UDP port 53 is blocked by local firewall | Ping 1.1.1.1 — if that works but DNS times out, ISP is throttling DNS | Contact ISP — their DNS resolver may be degraded | Enable DNS over HTTPS/TLS to bypass ISP interference | Restart router to reset DNS cache and forwarder state

symptom_text: layer 3 addressing mismatch detected
solutions: Check device IP and mask: ipconfig /all or ip addr | Identify gateway IP and its subnet | Verify device subnet contains the gateway IP (use subnet calculator) | Example correct config: device 192.168.1.50/24, gateway 192.168.1.1 | Set correct subnet mask: 255.255.255.0 for /24 networks | If using static IP, reconfigure with matching subnet | Check DHCP server subnet option — it may be misconfigured | Restart network after fixing subnet to apply changes

symptom_text: traceroute fails at first hop only
solutions: Verify gateway IP: route print (Windows) or ip route show (Linux) | Ping gateway: ping <gateway_ip> — confirm it is unreachable | Re-enter correct default gateway in network settings | Check if gateway device is powered on and its port is active | Restart your network adapter | Check routing table for stale or conflicting routes | Reset TCP/IP stack: netsh int ip reset (Windows) | Verify no firewall rule is blocking ICMP to gateway

symptom_text: cannot get valid ip from access point
solutions: Verify DHCP is enabled in router admin panel | Release and renew: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew | Power cycle router — 30s off clears DHCP server state | Check DHCP pool is not exhausted (add more addresses) | Temporarily assign a static IP to confirm it is a DHCP issue | Check if device is in a VLAN that can reach the DHCP server | Increase DHCP lease time in router settings | Reset router DHCP scope to defaults

symptom_text: wifi adapter not found by operating system
solutions: Open Device Manager — check for yellow ! on network adapter | Right-click adapter → Enable if disabled | Check physical cable connection — try a different cable | Update driver: Device Manager → Update driver | Uninstall adapter in Device Manager then reboot to reinstall | Try a different USB or PCIe NIC to isolate hardware failure | Run Windows Network Diagnostics or Linux ethtool -t eth0 | Replace NIC if driver reinstall does not resolve it

symptom_text: ip collision detected on LAN segment
solutions: Identify conflicting device: arp -a | look for duplicate MACs | Release IP: ipconfig /release (Windows) or sudo dhclient -r | Renew IP: ipconfig /renew or sudo dhclient | If using static IP, move it outside DHCP pool range | Set DHCP reservations in router to prevent future conflicts | Check DHCP pool size — expand if needed | Restart router to clear stale DHCP lease table | Reserve MAC-to-IP bindings for all static devices in router

symptom_text: ping google.com fails but ping 8.8.8.8 works
solutions: Change DNS to Google (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Flush DNS cache — macOS: sudo dscacheutil -flushcache | Test with nslookup google.com to confirm DNS failure | Restart network adapter to clear DNS state | Check router DNS settings in admin panel | Try alternate DNS: Quad9 (9.9.9.9) or OpenDNS (208.67.222.222) | Disable DNS-over-HTTPS temporarily to test

symptom_text: dns resolution succeeds only sometimes
solutions: Test DNS timeout: nslookup -timeout=5 google.com | Switch to a faster DNS server (1.1.1.1 is fastest globally) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Check if UDP port 53 is blocked by local firewall | Ping 1.1.1.1 — if that works but DNS times out, ISP is throttling DNS | Contact ISP — their DNS resolver may be degraded | Enable DNS over HTTPS/TLS to bypass ISP interference | Restart router to reset DNS cache and forwarder state

symptom_text: network unreachable error from ping command
solutions: Verify gateway IP: route print (Windows) or ip route show (Linux) | Ping gateway: ping <gateway_ip> — confirm it is unreachable | Re-enter correct default gateway in network settings | Check if gateway device is powered on and its port is active | Restart your network adapter | Check routing table for stale or conflicting routes | Reset TCP/IP stack: netsh int ip reset (Windows) | Verify no firewall rule is blocking ICMP to gateway

symptom_text: cannot ARP for gateway across subnet boundary
solutions: Check device IP and mask: ipconfig /all or ip addr | Identify gateway IP and its subnet | Verify device subnet contains the gateway IP (use subnet calculator) | Example correct config: device 192.168.1.50/24, gateway 192.168.1.1 | Set correct subnet mask: 255.255.255.0 for /24 networks | If using static IP, reconfigure with matching subnet | Check DHCP server subnet option — it may be misconfigured | Restart network after fixing subnet to apply changes

symptom_text: routing problem preventing gateway access
solutions: Verify gateway IP: route print (Windows) or ip route show (Linux) | Ping gateway: ping <gateway_ip> — confirm it is unreachable | Re-enter correct default gateway in network settings | Check if gateway device is powered on and its port is active | Restart your network adapter | Check routing table for stale or conflicting routes | Reset TCP/IP stack: netsh int ip reset (Windows) | Verify no firewall rule is blocking ICMP to gateway

symptom_text: recursive dns query timing out
solutions: Test DNS timeout: nslookup -timeout=5 google.com | Switch to a faster DNS server (1.1.1.1 is fastest globally) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Check if UDP port 53 is blocked by local firewall | Ping 1.1.1.1 — if that works but DNS times out, ISP is throttling DNS | Contact ISP — their DNS resolver may be degraded | Enable DNS over HTTPS/TLS to bypass ISP interference | Restart router to reset DNS cache and forwarder state

symptom_text: ip configuration failed dhcp not responding
solutions: Verify DHCP is enabled in router admin panel | Release and renew: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew | Power cycle router — 30s off clears DHCP server state | Check DHCP pool is not exhausted (add more addresses) | Temporarily assign a static IP to confirm it is a DHCP issue | Check if device is in a VLAN that can reach the DHCP server | Increase DHCP lease time in router settings | Reset router DHCP scope to defaults

solutions: Change DNS to Google (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) | Flush DNS cache — Windows: ipconfig /flushdns | Flush DNS cache — macOS: sudo dscacheutil -flushcache | Test with nslookup google.com to confirm DNS failure | Restart network adapter to clear DNS state | Check router DNS settings in admin panel | Try alternate DNS: Quad9 (9.9.9.9) or OpenDNS (208.67.222.222) | Disable DNS-over-HTTPS temporarily to test

symptom_text: network device unavailable or missing
solutions: Open Device Manager — check for yellow ! on network adapter | Right-click adapter → Enable if disabled | Check physical cable connection — try a different cable | Update driver: Device Manager → Update driver | Uninstall adapter in Device Manager then reboot to reinstall | Try a different USB or PCIe NIC to isolate hardware failure | Run Windows Network Diagnostics or Linux ethtool -t eth0 | Replace NIC if driver reinstall does not resolve it

symptom_text: cannot use this ip address it is taken
solutions: Identify conflicting device: arp -a | look for duplicate MACs | Release IP: ipconfig /release (Windows) or sudo dhclient -r | Renew IP: ipconfig /renew or sudo dhclient | If using static IP, move it outside DHCP pool range | Set DHCP reservations in router to prevent future conflicts | Check DHCP pool size — expand if needed | Restart router to clear stale DHCP lease table | Reserve MAC-to-IP bindings for all static devices in router
